slides.title:
Who Will Control the
AI Infrastructure
of Higher Education?
slides.subtitle: From useful tools to structural dependence, and how universities can still shape their future
slides.author: Dr Stuart Grey
slides.badge: Future Facing Learning and AI in Higher Education 2026

notes.transition: Open with the question, not with housekeeping.
points: Frame the keynote as a shift from 'how should we use AI?' to 'who controls the AI we use?'
You will hear talks about pedagogy, assessment, and practice.
This talk is neither anti-AI nor anti-industry; it is about governance.

voiceover.transcript: Hi, and good afternoon everyone. My name is Dr. Stuart Gray, and today I'll be talking to you about AI infrastructure, and in particular AI infrastructure in education. And we've seen lots of talks these next few days on pedagogy and assessment and practice. But what I would like to discuss today is a shift from how should we use AI to who controls the AI we use. And one of the things I'd like to point out is this talk is neither anti-AI nor anti-industry. It's about governance.

notes.transition: Move from the big question to why I am the person making this argument.
points: So, what's my angle?

voiceover.transcript: So why am I up here today talking to you all? Well there's two key reasons why I have a unique position with which to talk to AI infrastructure within higher education.

voiceover_history.transcript: So why listen to me? So there's two real parts about why I'm in a position to hopefully make this argument in a relatively persuasive manner.

notes.transition: Take the first half of that perspective: technical grounding.
points: Use this to show I have a machine learning / AI research background, not just a policy interest.
The point is that I understand AI systems as systems with architecture, coordination, and failure modes.

voiceover_history.transcript: The first is my technical background. I did a PhD in Distributed AI Systems. Since then, I've moved to use those tools of my career into more of my teaching and learning role. I also still teach part-time at University of Glasgow. I have the embedded academic experience and also the technical know-how. That domain knowledge and the technical know-how about how these models will work. I can create these models, test them, and understand hopefully a little bit about where they've been and a tiny bit about where they might go.

voiceover.transcript: The first reason here is my technical background. So I have a PhD in AI as applied to space systems. And this has given me a unique viewpoint on how this recent generative AI boom has rolled out and allows me to understand a little bit better, perhaps, the different failure modes, the strengths, the weaknesses, of the underlying technology.

slides.title: Free Text Analysis with Student Voice AI

notes.transition: Then pivot from technical grounding to applied institutional grounding.
points: Explain that most of my day-to-day work is free-text analysis with UK education institutions through Student Voice AI.
This means I see what AI and data practices look like inside real institutional workflows, not only in theory.
Say explicitly that the talk comes from looking at both the technology and the institutional consequences.

Reference: Student Voice AI homepage / customer evidence, showing Russell Group clients including Edinburgh, Leeds, King's, Warwick, UCL, LSE and Queen's University Belfast. https://www.studentvoice.ai/

Reference: Newcastle University partnership announcement, updated 6 March 2026, supporting the total of eight Russell Group universities mentioned in the talk. https://www.studentvoice.ai/blog/student-voice-and-university-of-newcastle-2023/

voiceover.transcript: The second reason is, as well as being a part-time senior lecturer at University of Glasgow, is I've used that technical background to found Student Voice AI, where we analyse free text comments for a wide range of UK institutions, including eight Russell Group institutions. So I have that balance of the technical knowledge and experience and the hard-won experience, as well as working with a number of UK universities on implementing these tools, with a view to improving the student experience. So that impact on the final user, right? The most important part of what we do in teaching and learning is focusing on the student and how these tools can be used to make that leap.

voiceover_history.transcript: And I've also founded and run a spin-out called Student Voice AI, where we analyze free text student comments for universities across the UK, including eight Russell Group universities. So again, this gives me a real insight into the university ecosystem, the current problems and issues around use of AI. So between those two, my academic background, the technical background, hopefully I can have something to say about where we are and where we're going.

notes.transition: Now give the room the first of three lenses for reading the rest of the talk.
points: Transparency asks: what can institutions actually see, inspect, explain, and contest?
Use it to frame provenance, auditability, model dependencies, data use, and institutional legibility.

voiceover_history.transcript: Now, throughout today's talk, I'd like you to keep three things in mind, if possible. We've got three kind of angles to what I'll be talking about in terms of AI infrastructure. And the first is transparency, as in, do we have any visibility into this infrastructure? As things become more and more important, the transparency becomes more and more important. And currently, we're relying on these models on trust. And I'll make some arguments about why perhaps that trust is misplaced, especially with some of these companies involved. So do we have access? Are we able to audit, explain what is coming into and out of these models? Are we able to see what training data was used to create them? Are we able to see what decisions were made around how they were post-trained, et cetera? So lots of things on transparency. So that's the first thing to bear in mind.

voiceover.transcript: So, I'd like you to bear in mind three key concepts when I'm going through my talk today. And the first of those is transparency. And by transparency, I mean, do we have any visibility into what's happening? Can we audit what's happening? Can we explain what's happening? Do we have end-to-end visibility of the supply chain, of the data that goes into these models, and the data that comes out, and the decisions that come out, right? I think that's really important. That's something we want to be touching on throughout the talk.

notes.transition: Add the second lens by shifting from visibility to shape.
points: Distortion asks what gets bent when systems reward what is easy to capture, measure, or automate.
This will later connect to assessment design, pedagogy, workflow simplification, and institutional incentives.

voiceover.transcript: The next concept is that of distortion, and there's two aspects to this. There's the one we're perhaps all familiar with, in that these models don't necessarily tell the truth. That's a given, putting it kindly. So we have to be careful where we're using them and what type of model we're using for which job we want to give it to, because these models are incredible. We are basically commoditizing some form of decision-making and intelligence here, which is a leap forward educationally, but we have to be really careful how it's used. The other aspect of distortion I'll lean into is our choice of platform and vendor will ultimately change and distort what we're able to do and how we're able to do it. So we're at a very key inflection point at the moment where any decisions we make will have an outsized impact in the possibilities of what we're able to do in the future, and we don't want to be sleepwalking into it. We can make decisions based on lots of evidence, right, and I won't argue any individual institutions, individual decision, but we shouldn't be doing it blind.

voiceover_history.transcript: The next is about distortion. And there's two aspects to this. The first is the thing we probably have heard a lot about is that these models don't tell the truth or any sort of objective truth, right? They are there, they're designed to interact with people, they're designed to continue to keep those interactions going and as such, things get distorted, right? They can be overly sycophantic, right? They can just hide certain things, not discuss certain things, right? And that could be a definite benefit. You don't want them explaining how to make a bomb or whatever, but you do need to understand how they are distorting things, what their limitations are, what their designs are making them do, and if that's the right decision for our use or not. The other type of distortion is as we engage with these tools and they become more infrastructure, it will distort our ways of working as well. And I think that's maybe slightly less discussed and I think that's something I would like to highlight throughout this talk.

notes.transition: Complete the triad with the political-economy lens.
points: Extraction asks who captures value when educational activity becomes data, workflow, or dependency.
Later this will connect to data use, vendor leverage, upstream value capture, and platform power.

voiceover_history.transcript: And the last of the three lenses is about extraction. So really it's about incentives. Who is making money off this? Who is paying lots of money for these tools? Who has assets that are worthwhile? Who is taking control of those assets? Who is deciding what is used and what's not used? And I think we can maybe get some hints there, the way I'm talking even, about why I think about that. But again, another third lens to talk about it. So we have transparency, distortion, and extraction.

voiceover.transcript: And the last concept is that of extraction. What value are we putting on the data we are generating, we are using to train these models, to evaluate these models? Are we giving it for free to large tech companies? What is being extracted by whom? Who are we paying for the privilege to use these models? And who is extracting the value from our work? And I will make, hopefully, a case that we should be in control of more of our destiny in terms of generating AI than just giving it wholesale to large tech companies.

slides.text: The argument in higher education is no longer about whether AI tools are impressive. It is about who decides what we can do with AI.
slides.highlight: It is about who decides what we can do with AI

notes.transition: Now make the frame explicit and turn the lenses into the keynote thesis.
points: Say that the argument is no longer mainly about whether the tools are impressive.
Say the deeper question is who sets the terms on which we use AI as AI becomes normal infrastructure.
Use this as the bridge from the opening frame into the body of the talk.

voiceover.transcript: So, there's a big discourse at the moment about what AI tools are good at or not, and you can pick holes in all sorts of things they do. They're not good at everything, but they are very good at a certain subset of items, and I think we'll find areas we're not currently using that will also be excellent at if we use the models correctly with the right kinds of data, but I'm not worried about what they're good at or bad at. What I'm worried about is who decides, who gets to decide who can use AI within an institution. What AI is used, what are the boundaries, right? We are research institutions as well. Are we able to push and make changes ourselves, or are we stuck with whatever is defined by a vendor?

voiceover_history.transcript: And an important part of what I'd like to do is to skip beyond the arguments about whether these tools are impressive or useful in any given context, because I think we can all hopefully list areas where they are very useful, right? Unless you're really diehard anti-AI, in which case, okay, it's a very different discussion we're having there. But I think they can be seen to be useful for certain tasks. But that's not what I'm talking about, right? I want to talk about who sets the terms on which we use this AI. Who decides how we can use it? Who decides what it's useful for, right? And that maps across all of our three angles.

slides.title: From tool to infrastructure
slides.subtitle: How AI moved from optional assistance to background infrastructure

notes.transition: Having set up the lenses, move into the first descriptive claim.
points: This section explains how AI moved from visible tool to background infrastructure.
Use the subtitle to tell the audience we are now moving from framing to diagnosis.
The job here is to make 'infrastructure' feel concrete.

voiceover.transcript: So, in this first section, I want to talk about that journey from, okay, these are useful tools that everyone started playing with a few years ago. How does it go from that to being part of the infrastructure that we could perhaps get trapped in, right? How we go from a nice tool we're playing with in a web page to vendor lock-in.

voiceover_history.transcript: So, in this first section, I'd like to talk about what happens when something goes from a useful tool we are using, a very specific use case, to becoming part of infrastructure. And we can look back at some examples of this and hopefully think about ourselves about we can see this happening in real time, but what are the issues and what should we be aware of in this case?

slides.heading: AI is moving from visible tools to invisible systems
slides.body: The important shift is not simply more campus use. It is that AI is being stitched into teaching, assessment, student support, research workflows and institutional decision-making faster than governance is catching up. When that happens, AI stops looking like a product choice and starts behaving like infrastructure.

notes.transition: Start with the broad institutional picture before going to evidence.
points: AI is no longer just a tool someone opens; it is becoming part of how teaching, assessment, support, research, and governance are carried out.
Stress both the shift from optional use to embedded workflow and the lag in institutional coordination.
Say that once many activities assume the system is there, AI stops behaving like a product choice and starts behaving like infrastructure.
Teaching and assessment: Generative tools now sit inside drafting, feedback, marking support and redesign of assessment.
Student support and admin: Copilots, triage systems and analytics are creeping into advising, helpdesks and routine services.
Research and governance: Search, synthesis, coding, grant development and management reporting increasingly run through AI-mediated layers.

voiceover.transcript: So we've seen that transition from okay you go to chat GPT, you copy and paste things in and out, it's a very separate thing and that was used very early on for teaching assessment, for support, administration, it's used across the board in research as well, but we've got to be really clear what that journey is because as we move from okay you can copy and paste, okay it becomes more integrated in the tool, there's a button, the co-pilot button in Word for instance, to that journey to as you're doing work automatically things are processed, scanned and notification sent, right, there's a clear trajectory there as these tools become more mature and we don't want to be blindly sleepwalking to that.

voiceover_history.transcript: So, when we first started using these tools, I think ChatGPT was the quintessential initial moment. Okay, it's a chat box. It's a tool you go to. You can copy and paste things in, but it's a very separate entity. And this had real use, okay, across teaching and assessment, across student support and admin, research and governance, for right or wrong, right, especially the early versions, right? Major issues, but there were obvious use cases where you could copy something from some work into ChatGPT, get it to reword it, put it back, ask it for ideas, right, but it's a very separate thing. But what we see now are this movement from that specific use, that sort of encapsulated siloed use, to it kind of permitting in the background. It just exists now. And you draft something and it just pops up, okay, here's a new draft. There's a little button in the tools you're using. There are options to use it in different LTIs, in VLEs, in all the office suites we use, right? And it's permeating the whole thing and it becomes part of the substrate. That's a really important movement because this is a time, once that happens, it's just baked in and we don't have any choice anymore. We can't decide to go between ChatGPT and Claude anymore. You have to use whatever's built in. And we're finding this already with some of the co-pilot use institutions. So as things become more deliberate and made out of choice, they become more automatic and defined by whoever makes that decision once, and then that's the only way it can happen. And that is really dangerous.

slides.heading: Student adoption is already mainstream
slides.body: The governance problem is immediate because student practice has already normalised faster than institutional structures have adapted.

stats.label: of UK full-time undergraduates use AI in at least one way
use generative AI to help with assessed work
say assessment has changed significantly because of AI

notes.transition: Back the previous claim with numbers so the room feels the urgency.
points: Use the HEPI figures to show this is already mainstream student practice.
Link the assessment figure directly to the conference context: the room already knows assessment is changing.
The point is not surprise at student use; the point is that institutional governance can no longer lag behind it.
Reference: HEPI, Student Generative Artificial Intelligence Survey 2026, 12 March 2026. https://www.hepi.ac.uk/reports/student-generative-ai-survey-2026/
Reference: Survey conducted by Savanta in December 2025, based on 1,054 full-time UK undergraduates. https://www.hepi.ac.uk/reports/student-generative-ai-survey-2026/

Reference: HEPI reports 95% use AI in at least one way, 94% use generative AI to help with assessed work, and 65% say assessment has changed significantly because of AI. https://www.hepi.ac.uk/reports/student-generative-ai-survey-2026/

voiceover_history.transcript: Now, to put some numbers on this adoption in that the use of the tool has skyrocketed. Vast majority of UK undergraduates use AI in at least some way. They've used it with assessed work, and most people agree that assessment has changed significantly because of AI. Again, mostly for the worst, but sometimes for the better. There are other options there. But this tidal wave is happening, and that was just with the standalone tools. This is before we bake it into everything we're doing, which is slowly, and I think perhaps inevitably happening. You have to decide, okay, if this is actually happening, what are we going to do about it? Where do we say no? Where do we say, okay, we'll do something different? Or where do you say, okay, no, that's actually fine? Where's the governance here?

voiceover.transcript: So, I don't want to dwell on numbers too much, but student use is basically across the board. Now, there are cases of students not wanting to engage with it, which is absolutely fine for moral or philosophical reasons, and likewise with staff. But the usage is there, and it's growing. These tools are everywhere because they have real utility. Now, people are using them in the wrong way quite frequently, on the wrong thing, and using the wrong tool for the job, and all sorts of different things, right? But when they are well-aligned with a problem, they are excellent, and that is why this transition from tool to infrastructure is happening, because there is real utility there. So, people are building into things, so we have to be on it as that build-out happens to have some control and ownership of that process.

slides.heading: Universities need a viable system for AI governance, not just a policy
slides.body: Many institutions already have local experimentation. What they often lack is coordination, assurance, foresight and mission-level steering across the whole system, especially when teaching continuity depends on external platforms.

notes.transition: Before leaving the opening diagnosis, turn it into the first institutional implication.
points: If governance is lagging behind use, the answer cannot be a thin guidance document.
The institution needs coordination, assurance, foresight and mission-level steering across the whole system.
Use this as the first practical move so the talk does not leave action until the end.
Coordinate the edge: Join up local experimentation so teaching, support, procurement and data decisions are not scattered.
Build assurance: Test tools, document risk and create routes for review and contestation before high-stakes use.
Steer by mission: Make senior governance articulate what kind of university the AI stack is supposed to serve.

voiceover.transcript: And that's why we don't necessarily need more policies on who should do what with generative AI. We need more governance, which to me means more teeth, as in more structure. This is what we're doing, right? This is what we're checking, right? And as a systems engineer, as a background, I think a key theme running through this talk is the need for verification, validation. What are we doing? Why are we actually checking that this thing is useful, whatever use case it might be? Are we bringing intellectual rigor to this? And to do this, we need to be very coordinated across the institution, right? Different stakeholders in teaching and learning, in research, in admin, in procurement have to come together. We have to build the capability to be sure it's doing the right thing, which I don't believe we currently have. And we have to make sure it's all being steered by our fundamental mission as an institution to teach students, right? To carry out cutting edge research, right? And operate as a for-good public body.

voiceover_history.transcript: And this is where we get to the difference between governance and policy. Now, all institutions, rightly, I'm not picking on anyone here, are producing AI policies, right? Lots of hand-wringing and lots of hard work by everyone. But we don't need policies because what we need is that step beyond policy, which is governance, which is policy with teeth, right? With action, with autonomy built in, right? Which means coordination between different users. We need assurance, testing. And I'll be building on this a lot as we go on through this talk. How are we sure this is doing what we do? Where are the tests? I'm a systems engineer by training. How are we testing this? How are we verifying it? How are we validating it? And those two are very specific things. And perhaps most importantly, how are we making sure that it's all going towards the mission of our higher education institutions? Now, they will be different by institution, but generally they are for the societal good, for education and research, right? Quite noble, I think. And we have to make sure that that is baked into all the decisions. And that's a governance issue, not a policy issue, right? The policy can say what it likes or what you should and shouldn't do. But governance is making sure what actually happens on the ground and pushing in those directions, okay?

slides.title: The bubble
slides.subtitle: Why the boom matters even if the hype does not last

notes.transition: Having sketched the first implication, zoom back out to the upstream build-out behind it.
points: This section moves from campus use to the political economy of the wider stack.
Signal that the next slides are not dismissing AI as hype; they are asking what durable capacity the boom is creating, and for whom.
This is the bridge from institutional dependence to the infrastructure being built upstream.

voiceover.transcript: So, we talked about the pervasive use of these tools, and this has been a very rapid, rapid expansion, and there's lots of talk of the AI bubble, right, in terms of tons and tons of money being put into AI. And the next section here is talking about, okay, so what are the implications of that for higher education, right? We're not investing in these companies. Most of them are private. We couldn't if we wanted to, and I wouldn't want to invest in them. Picking winners is very difficult, but there is definitely a bubble, or if you're being more kind, a boom of investment happening in these tools. So what does that mean for us?

voiceover_history.transcript: So what's the backdrop to this scenario? So we can take a step back. And I think we can all agree, we're in some sort of AI bubble, right? Now, if you're being unkind, you call it a bubble. If you're being kind, you call it a boom, right? Both the same thing. A absolutely tremendous amount of money is being spent rolling out these systems and to train them and to inference, okay, so give the answers to people. And I think the scale of it is really important. And I think we've got to understand that scale, where we sit within that and where that relationship is and where we can go from there.

slides.heading: Tech companies are building capacity universities may later inherit
slides.body: The current AI boom is not just hype. It is also an enormous build-out of compute, models, tooling and organisational knowledge that may outlast the frenzy. If universities are strategic, some of that capacity can become cheaper access, better tools and more credible public-interest alternatives.

notes.transition: Open the section by separating hype from infrastructure.
points: The key line is that speculative capital can still build real capacity.
Point to compute, models, tooling and organisational learning as durable inheritances of the current boom.
Say that the question is not whether infrastructure is being built; it is who will control what is left behind.
Compute and tooling: Data centres, GPU fleets, orchestration frameworks and evaluation tooling are being hardened quickly.
Open models and hardware: Maturing open models and post-boom hardware markets can make sector alternatives more plausible over time.
Institutional learning: Universities are also learning fast about policy, pedagogy and workflow design while the ecosystem is moving.

References: Synergy Research Group, Q4 2025 cloud market update; Alphabet Investor Relations, 2025 Q4 Earnings Call, 4 February 2026; Reuters factbox on major AI infrastructure commitments. https://www.srgresearch.com/articles/genai-helps-drive-quarterly-cloud-revenues-to-119-billion-as-growth-rate-jumped-yet-again-in-q4 https://abc.xyz/investor/events/event-details/2026/2025-Q4-Earnings-Call-2026-Dr_C033hS6/default.aspx https://www.tradingview.com/news/reuters.com%2C2025%3Anewsml_L3N3V91EW%3A0-companies-pouring-billions-to-advance-ai-infrastructure/

voiceover.transcript: Well, I'm actually very positive about this AI bubble. It might seem a bit strange. Now, there will be problems when the bubble inevitably bursts. There will be stock market crashes, et cetera. A large amount of a lot of stock market indices is based around these big tech companies, a lot of circular financing, for instance. But all that aside, when the bubble bursts, there will be some things left over. And we can look to history for examples of this, where there's, when you think of a bubble, people frequently think of tulips in the Netherlands. But that's actually a very unique example of no real utility being associated with the output of that bubble. There's many more through history examples of bubbles leaving behind real infrastructure. Now, there are lots of winners and losers, mostly losers during the actual bubble itself, but what's left is useful. Think of railroads. Lots of competing companies building railways, most of which don't exist anymore, but the railways exist, okay? Likewise with telephone networks or telegram networks. Lots of competition, lots of investment, only a few winners, but what's left is real infrastructure. So we're left with compute and tooling. We're left with open source models. Again, I don't have much time to go into that today, but what I use in my own work in student voice AI is a lot of the open source LLM models, which we can run on our own hardware. It means we're not sending the data anywhere else. We're not training anyone else's models. We're using our own models to have full control and full visibility of what we're doing. Also, we've got this opportunity. As this bubble expands, we're getting a lot of institutional learning. So if we're doing anything else, we should be keeping our heads up, looking at what is useful, what's not, be interested in other people's use case because as this bubble expands and eventually pops, we need to have that understanding of what is a good use for this, what should we keep once the companies go away, and what should we just disregard and was always useless.

voiceover_history.transcript: Now, this AI bubble is huge. Billions of dollars are being wasted because not everyone can win. Not everyone can possibly make enough money back to pay off the investors for these things. So a lot of money will be lost. But I honestly think it's a good thing, right? Bubbles can have real benefits. Now, when everyone talks about, okay, what's an example of a financial bubble, people go to tulips, right? In the 1600s. But that's actually a very remarkably different type of bubble than most, right? That is a purely financial bubble. That could be equated maybe with cryptocurrency, right? They're all on a par. The vast majority of bubbles we've had before have actually pushed forward significantly the technology and the ability to do things. So telecommunications, okay, bubbles building out loads of hardware for telephones, for internet, right? Railroads, okay? Tremendous amounts of investment needed and lots of companies have bust. But what they left behind was railways. What they left behind was phone networks. So what's going to be left behind? There'll be no love lost if Anthropic and OpenAI go tits up and were left with some different companies. I don't care. But we're left with a whole lot of infrastructure in terms of compute and tooling at data centers, right? In terms of open models and software and hardware that goes together. And really importantly, we'll be left with a lot of institutional learning, right? We're going through this, we're learning to use these tools whether we like it or not. We, if we are smart, can use this as an opportunity to decide which aspects we want to use and where, right? Using consumer, and this is a key point I want to make, using consumer grade tools in an educational research setting is stupid because they're not designed for that, right? They're designed for chatting about, okay, what should I do? I've got three hours in Middlesbrough, what would be the main sites to see, okay?

slides.heading: The upstream build-out is vast — and highly concentrated
slides.body: The boom is leaving behind real infrastructure, but that infrastructure is being financed and controlled by a remarkably small set of actors.

stats.label: top three providers' share of the public cloud market in Q4 2025
Alphabet's guided 2026 capital expenditure range, with most CapEx invested in technical infrastructure
announced multi-year AI infrastructure deals and project ceilings shaping the frontier stack

notes.transition: Now show the scale and concentration numerically.
points: Use these figures to make the upstream stack feel vast and unevenly owned.
The point is not memorising exact numbers; it is seeing how far beyond single-university scale this market operates.
This helps explain why universities are mostly downstream actors unless they collaborate strategically.

Reference: Synergy Research Group, Q4 2025 public cloud market share update, 5 February 2026; the top three providers held 68% of the public cloud market in Q4 2025. https://www.srgresearch.com/articles/genai-helps-drive-quarterly-cloud-revenues-to-119-billion-as-growth-rate-jumped-yet-again-in-q4

Reference: Alphabet Investor Relations, 2025 Q4 Earnings Call, 4 February 2026; 2026 CapEx guidance is $175bn-$185bn, with most CapEx invested in technical infrastructure. https://abc.xyz/investor/events/event-details/2026/2025-Q4-Earnings-Call-2026-Dr_C033hS6/default.aspx

Reference: Reuters factbox on major AI infrastructure commitments including Stargate (up to $500bn), OpenAI-Oracle ($300bn), and Nvidia-OpenAI (up to $100bn), supporting the rough ~$1tn framing. https://www.tradingview.com/news/reuters.com%2C2025%3Anewsml_L3N3V91EW%3A0-companies-pouring-billions-to-advance-ai-infrastructure/

voiceover_history.transcript: And I don't want to dwell on this, but the actual money being spent and the scale of this build out and the boom is absolutely spectacular. So just three quick examples. Alphabet guided for 175 to 185 billion dollars of capital expenditure in 2026, mostly for technical infrastructure. 68% of the public cloud market in Q4 2025 was held by the top three providers. Hi AWS, sponsor of this conference. And there is around a trillion dollars in announced multi-year AI infrastructure deals and project ceilings shaping the frontier stack. Now, a lot of this money is entirely circular, which means that when the buck stops, companies will collapse. But again, I don't care. The stock market will care and probably care a little bit, but fundamentally, what's left over will be useful. These data centers will still be useful.

voiceover.transcript: So, I'd like to give you a very quick idea of the scale of the investment made by these very small number of companies. We've got the hyperscalers, who are like Microsoft, Amazon, Google, and we've also got the new AI Frontier Labs, so OpenAI, Anthropic. And all of this build-out is really concentrated on those companies. And we are talking in the hundreds of billions of annual investments. So, Google's, or Alphabet, the parent company, its investment for 2026 is about $180 billion. Meta is going big for it, too. And across the industry, we're talking on the order of $1 trillion of hardware investment. So, there's a ton of investment in new data centers. And what's happening is this new hardware is being built, but the old hardware still exists. It might not be just quite as fast or as efficient. So, as we go through this bubble, this build-out process, there will be a real hardware boon for users who are willing to, okay, we have the expertise to build our own models, to deploy our own models. We can get generations back hardware, and we could be in a really good position.

slides.heading: But infrastructure built in a frenzy can still produce dependency
slides.body: Build-outs do not guarantee healthy outcomes. The same boom that creates useful capacity can also create habits of lock-in, misallocated effort and narrow models of progress.

notes.transition: Then add the caution.
points: A boom can create dependency as easily as it creates capability.
Emphasise generosity, convenience, and rapid integration as the conditions under which lock-in often begins.
This is the place to say that infrastructure built in a frenzy often embeds someone else's priorities.
Lock-in during generosity: Deep discounts and rapid integrations make dependence feel painless while switching costs are low and still rising.
Misplaced priorities: Leaders can chase visible partnerships and shiny features while neglecting pedagogy, people and core digital resilience.
Capture of norms: The first systems to scale often end up defining what counts as modern, efficient or inevitable.

voiceover.transcript: So that might have been a perhaps surprising start to that, okay, there's real benefit to the AI boom, but there also are real problems. Now again, I'm not going to dwell on externalities, which are there economically for when this bubble does burst inevitably, but talk about, okay, how do these tech companies, especially talking about open AI topic, how do companies like this normally work? And they normally, because they have all this investment, they're trying to grab market share as quickly as possible. And they are very generous, right? All these companies are serving these tokens, right? Serving these API models, these chatbots at a loss to try and get customers. And that generosity isn't just for generosity's sake, it's to get that customer base to then lock them in. And this means at this stage, but throughout the whole process, the priorities, the incentives are not aligned. They are just looking to get customers. They will do whatever it takes. They are not looking to produce the best product. They're not looking to give the best outcome for students. They're looking to get potential customers. And the prize here is that companies who can get a larger market share can set the terms, can basically capture the norms of the market, what was expected, what was possible, what do people understand as possible, whereas I think there's a much wider range of things we could do with these tools if we're free with them. But these companies want to obviously constrain that, and there's a real, real tension here between either consumer models or more enterprising models, and we're stuck in the middle, right? A consumer model is very friendly, have a nice chat. It's great if you're asking, okay, what are five things to do on my trip to Venice, right? It'll give you great answers. The enterprise models will be good for running through all the TPS reports in a SharePoint. But when it comes to using these models for teaching and learning, they're not going to focus on that. But the norms might be set as the only things it can do are those two, so we have to be really careful there.

voiceover_history.transcript: So that's a really positive aspect of the bubble, but what we also have with the bubble because there's so much money flying in people, these companies, are selling these things below cost and that's really important to know, right? There's a lot of leaders, right? And they're being very generous, they're giving away, burning all this energy, burning money basically, right, to try and get people locked into their ecosystem. And this means there's lots of opportunities for misplaced priorities. They're trying to please everyone, they're trying to get everyone locked in, they will promise the world, but things are not designed for specific use cases. And in our world, the HE sector seems like everything, but it's a small fry compared to larger business world and the larger consumer world. So we'll get kind of left out. And we don't want either a business model, and we don't really want a consumer model. We want our own higher education specific approaches. The priorities are not the same. But what happens is, as these huge companies are spending all this money, the norms are set, right? What expectations are what is possible and what's not. And coming from a technical background, that's a real shame, because these models offer tremendous opportunity if we want to design and use them ourselves, right, to do what we want to do. But if it's just going to be a consumer chatbot or enterprise chatbot, that's not any use to us.

notes.transition: Now move from capacity to power.
points: This section asks who actually sets the defaults embedded in the stack.
Use the subtitle to signal that the issue is not only lock-in but agenda-setting and mission drift.
We are moving from 'how much is being built?' to 'whose assumptions shape what gets built?'

voiceover.transcript: So who gets to set these defaults is a fundamental question and we could just sleepwalk into letting these companies do it, or we could do it. It's about taking control, getting the power to make the decisions, but power has to be taken, right? We have to be proactive. These companies are not just going to give us what we want because fundamentally there's a huge market for the commercial AI, a huge market for enterprise AI. The education market to actually do good pedagogy and have these tools help teach people is very small, so they're not going to concentrate on it. So it's up to us to decide to set that. We have to really take ownership and try and take control of that process.

voiceover_history.transcript: So that leads me on to who gets to set these defaults, who decides it, and that is people, that is users, right? We have the opportunity to help set the defaults and I'll talk about who is currently setting them and hopefully how we can start to set them.

slides.heading: A small number of firms increasingly control every layer
slides.body: AI is not arriving in higher education as a neutral marketplace of interchangeable tools. A concentrated set of firms controls key model, cloud and interface layers, leaving universities mostly downstream users of systems shaped elsewhere.

notes.transition: Start with the stack itself.
points: Explain that universities are not buying from a flat market of interchangeable tools.
Show the layered dependence: model providers, cloud providers, and interface/platform providers.
Use the scale asymmetry to explain why universities are usually downstream even when they are important customers.
Model layer: A handful of providers dominate frontier model access and shape the pace of release.
Infrastructure and interface: Cloud concentration and campus platforms together shape cost, continuity, distribution and defaults.
Scale asymmetry: Universities will rarely win by outspending the frontier, so leverage has to come from strategy, collaboration and governance.

Reference: Synergy Research Group, Q4 2025 public cloud market share update; the top three providers held 68% of the public cloud market in Q4 2025. https://www.srgresearch.com/articles/genai-helps-drive-quarterly-cloud-revenues-to-119-billion-as-growth-rate-jumped-yet-again-in-q4

Reference: Alphabet Investor Relations, 2025 Q4 Earnings Call, 4 February 2026; 2026 CapEx guidance is $175bn-$185bn. https://abc.xyz/investor/events/event-details/2026/2025-Q4-Earnings-Call-2026-Dr_C033hS6/default.aspx

voiceover.transcript: With a small number of companies controlling this AI stack, if you would call it that, we're not picking from a free and open marketplace of interchangeable options. They're all very tightly integrated, and this is by design, and they want you to buy into their vision of, okay, this is all this set of models from one provider, there's different tooling structures from that provider, and this is very difficult to compete against because once they've locked in, you're using all their tools and products, right, they've got you. And we can't create our own version of that in entirety. It's not realistic because of the scale asymmetry in that, as I mentioned before, the amount of investment needed to actually run these models is huge, so we've got to try and take a different tack to decide, okay, who's going to make the decisions for us, who takes control, who has power in this relationship with these AI providers.

voiceover_history.transcript: There's a very small number of firms that are controlling this AI stack, the hardware, the software, the inference, all these sort of things. These firms control everything, and we are purely downstream users. We are not going to be competing with them on scale. That's kind of why I went through all that investment. We do not have 175 to 185 billion dollars a year to invest in hardware, and nor should we. Our aim should be to use the hardware that will be left in the wayside, okay, one or two generations old, that'll be perfectly good for what we want to do, and use that. We shouldn't be trying to compete with them, right? We're not starting our own AI frontier lab. We're thinking about, okay, how can these tools, these approaches, all this hardware that's been built out, be used for our own ends?
You

So, a small number of companies increasingly control different aspects of this AI tooling stack, if you want to call it that, and there's a few different layers there. There's the sort of models themselves. These front-end models are huge and very expensive to run, but there's a whole suite of different sizes of models, and the very small ones that are run on a smartphone, and who controls these is up for grabs. There's currently companies releasing open-source versions, but also there's lots of closed-source versions too. Also, the infrastructure... I don't like that.

slides.heading: The real question is whose goals the technology serves
slides.body: Technology does not choose its own social purpose. In universities, the central question is whose goals are being encoded when AI is sold as productivity, innovation or student experience.

notes.transition: Then make the normative move.
points: Technology does not arrive with its social purpose already settled.
Stress that who decides the purpose of a system matters as much as what the system can do.
Bring the argument fully into higher education by asking whose goals are being encoded.
Direction matters: AI can be used to remove drudgery and expand human capability, or to centralise control and reduce labour.
Competing incentives: Management, vendor and platform incentives overlap imperfectly with educational purpose.
Academic and student interests: Universities are supposed to privilege learning, judgment, accessibility, care and public value.

voiceover_history.transcript: That's where we come back again to the incentives and the direction. So what these tools are for is super important. A general purpose tool doesn't exist. These tools are designed for certain purposes. They are good at lots of different things. I can give an exam paper to a consumer model, and it will do a good job. That's not the point, because we're not looking for answers. We're looking for, OK, how well learning is being grappled with those ILOs in that exam, for instance. It's a totally different type of question. And it might be subtle to those outside. But in HE, it's super, super important. And the incentives are not aligned. There are incentives by these companies to sell and to get people using these tools, especially university. It's why historically big companies like Adobe, et cetera, always gave free licenses to universities to get students using these tools and use them in their work life later on. And we don't want that to happen here. And also, in terms of academic and student interests, it doesn't map in that we want very different things. We're not looking for the end result. We're looking for something to help us with the process. And that is a fundamentally different approach that isn't tackled with these consumer models.

voiceover.transcript: So, the first question we have to try and tackle is what is the purpose of these models? We have to bring our institutional purpose to bear, right? Because that fundamental purpose, that direction is super important because currently the models are designed for either consumer use or more enterprise business use. We have to say our models need to be our approaches, our systems, our tools have to be able to fulfill our desires as a university. And there will be tension there with those providers and we have to accept that and we have to maybe put up some fights and say no to some things, right? I think that's very clear because if you want to keep student interests, academic interests, research, admin, what makes universities special, we have to put up a fight and say we're not using all these tools just for everything in our job. We have to take a different approach.

slides.heading: Procurement teams set the agenda
slides.body: The deepest risk is not only lock-in but agenda-setting. Mission drift enters through reasonable purchases that gradually rewrite what counts as normal teaching infrastructure, research workflow and institutional analytics.

notes.transition: Deepen the risk from lock-in to agenda-setting.
points: Mission drift usually arrives through sensible local decisions, not dramatic declarations.
Bundled convenience, default pathways, and contract terms rewrite the environment gradually.
This is where the talk becomes clearly about power, not just procurement.
Bundled convenience: AI arrives inside familiar productivity suites and platforms, making deeper dependency feel incremental.
Default pathways: Institution-wide licences quietly define which tools staff and students see as normal or approved.
Contractual gravity: Once workflows, content and reporting are built into one stack, future decisions become less free than they appear.

voiceover.transcript: And we have to be really clear about how these decisions are made, because again, having been in university for many years and also selling to universities through student voice AI, these decisions are frequently made in procurement. So if we are looking to make decisions about what we want to buy for, do we want an across-the-board tool we're going to use everywhere, or are we going to pick and choose? I would strongly urge for the latter. We have to talk to colleagues in procurement and make sure we're all on the same page, because otherwise they, with the best intentions, will try to get the best deal from vendors based on their criteria we give them. And if we give them duff criteria, we are snookered, because then we get big contracts, it becomes a default or university, and very hard to break out of. These contracts are long, and it's far more likely the contract's just extended, because everyone's built on this one sweat of very vertically integrated tools. So we have to be really careful at this stage with our procurement colleagues, and give them the best deal possible to help procure what we need from these companies, but also perhaps build what we need to build ourselves.

voiceover_history.transcript: So we have to try and set the agenda, but the agenda is normally actually set via procurement. So we have to be really careful and really clear with our procurement teams, I'm sure some procurement colleagues here, about what is needed, what's required, what we're buying, because what we procure becomes the default. It becomes the whole envelope of possibilities. Because once we're in there, the defaults are set, these contracts are long, they're expensive, they'll probably be renewed because, okay, we can't move off. We have to think about procurement and not lock ourselves in to one way of thinking. We have to really try and be sensible of what we're trying to do as a university and why we're trying to do it.

slides.text: If universities buy into a stack on vendor terms, the infrastructure is decided before it's debated.
slides.highlight: infrastructure is decided before it's debated

notes.transition: Let the central warning crystallise.
points: This is one of the strongest lines in the deck, so give it air.
The point is that infrastructure settles before institutions have fully debated its consequences.
Use it to introduce a procurement implication: the way universities buy now will shape the future they are still able to contest.

voiceover.transcript: And again, just to reiterate that point, if we make these decisions about what infrastructure, if we are doing these things on the vendor's terms, we're basically admitting defeat. Because then that underlying substrate, that infrastructure, the AI infrastructure is decided before we get a chance to even debate internally what is it good for, what is it not good for, what should we build, what should we buy.

voiceover_history.transcript: So just to rest on that point, we have to decide the terms, not the vendors. So procurement, they become our absolute heroes, our champions. They have to push for what we want as universities, rather than what is being offered, right? And if what is being offered doesn't match it, we say, no, thank you. Right? We can go a different path, right? Which we'll talk about at the end of this talk.

notes.transition: After the procurement implication, move back into what dependence actually does inside the university.
points: This section asks what dependence changes in pedagogy, data and educational purpose.
Signal that the next slides are about educational consequences, not just market structure.
This is where the three opening lenses start paying off most clearly.

voiceover.transcript: So why would this be a bad thing? Why is doing these things on the vendor terms so, so bad? What's so wrong with being dependent on one of these AI labs models? Again, they're excellent. So what's the problem? You know, ChatGPT and Claude can do incredible things. So what's the problem with buying into one of these stacks wholeheartedly?

voiceover_history.transcript: Okay, so in the next section, I'd like to talk a little bit about more what this dark path, what this look and dependence, what we sleepwalk into living on vendor terms, what does that look like?

slides.heading: The crucial divide is between augmentation and substitution
slides.body: The most important design decision is not whether AI is present. It is whether AI is used to amplify human expertise or to thin it out.

notes.transition: Open with the central distinction.
points: The most important divide is augmentation versus substitution.
Say that the question is not whether AI is present, but what kind of relationship to human expertise the institution is normalising.
This becomes the test you keep applying to the rest of the examples.
Augmentation: AI can remove admin burden, scaffold formative feedback and support accessibility so staff can focus on judgment and contact.
Substitution: AI can also be used to replace teaching presence, automate first-pass support and standardise assessment in thinner ways.
Institutional test: Every use case should be judged by whether it improves educational experience and professional autonomy, not merely cost.

voiceover.transcript: Well, it depends what you're trying to do. And they have, again, very different incentives around what they're trying to encourage you to do. But fundamentally, there's two broad classes of the type of work AI could do. And that's augmentation, helping us do what we want to do, helping us ideate, helping us clean up our inbox, organize the files on our desktop, whatever it might be. And then there's substitution, which is, OK, a job role that was there is now being replaced by AI. So rather than having a discussion with a student, it's an AI chatbot, things like that. And they are very, very different outcomes, right? And what we have to do is we have to be very careful with what we're deciding to augment. Some things don't need any augmentation at all. Some things could really benefit from it. And some things could be substituted. Some things is work no one wants to do. But we have to look at this through an institutional lens, not through the vendor lens of, OK, yeah, in a company, this could be automated. Well, perhaps we don't want to do that, because, again, we have to understand what our value is as a university and lean into that.

voiceover_history.transcript: First, I'll have to clarify different uses and types of AI use. First of all, there's augmentation, having these models and systems help us do what we want to do, enable us to do our more human things. And there are lots and lots of possibilities. And I'm not at all anti-AI, I'm very pro-AI. But I think it's got to be used in the very right way. What's more troublesome is pure substitution, when whole aspects of our educational system and our research system are being replaced, end-to-end, black box, that's really, really quite worrying. Because what we are not doing is this institutional test of, is this what we need to do as our institution? Not in terms of efficiency, in terms of jobs to be done, but in terms of our philosophy. Is this what we need? We will have to change. But are we going to change, in a way, the maps to our principles? Because I think anyone who wants to keep a university from 10, 20 years ago, it's not going to happen. Universities will change. But we can still change while still keeping our principles there, because we adapt to these new tools.

slides.heading: The easiest uses to scale are often the least educationally valuable
slides.body: Universities under pressure are especially vulnerable to AI uses that look efficient because they industrialise surface tasks. The more defensible uses usually complement human work instead of trying to replace it.

notes.transition: Now name the tempting failure mode.
points: The most tempting uses are attractive because they scale quickly under financial and workload pressure.
But the better uses are usually the harder ones: admin relief, accessibility and formative scaffolding that preserve human judgment.
Say this is where distortion shows up most clearly.
Substitution at scale: Industrialised content, automated feedback and surveillance systems can look efficient while thinning educational value.
Augmentation where it counts: Admin relief, accessibility support and formative scaffolds are often more defensible because they complement human work.
Institutional test: Ask whether the use deepens educational experience and professional autonomy, not merely whether it is cheap to scale.

voiceover.transcript: And this is a problem because what is easy to scale and looks good in a demo is frequently the least educationally valuable, right? We could automate the whole thing, right? We could get ChatGPT or Claude to do end-to-end teaching to students, and it would be absolutely crap, right? I think we can all agree that. So where we have this institutional test of what's the educational value, we have to be really careful of, okay, what are these tools actually good for, right? And we're not just racing to the end. We're not speed running education. We're trying to look at where is a value in education, where's the value we bring, and where do these tools augment that rather than substitute it.

voiceover_history.transcript: It's especially dangerous when we start to push out the substitution aspect at scale, right? Especially if there's cost savings involved. Again, finances are pretty dire across the sector. In many cases, there'll be a desire for, okay, where can we save money? And that is a really dangerous road. You have to be very careful with what we replace with AI. Because what we can do is augment and make better what we do, right? That is definitely a possibility. But again, we have to meet the institutional test of, does this match with our institutional principles?

slides.heading: Pedagogy starts to bend around what the platform can measure
slides.body: Once AI tools are tightly integrated, course design can start adapting to the system rather than the other way round. That is how commercial logic becomes pedagogic logic.

notes.transition: Bring the distortion lens directly into pedagogy.
points: Platforms nudge teaching toward what they can count, standardise, and render visible.
Say that over time pedagogy can start adapting to platform logic rather than educational purpose.
This is a subtle but central claim: metrics and affordances become curricular pressure.
Standardisation pressure: Assessments and activities may drift toward formats that are easy to auto-score, summarise or monitor.
Surface metrics: Completion, engagement and usage are privileged because they are measurable, even when they are weak proxies for learning.
Platform visibility: Platforms and publishers can become the default route through which content, prompts and resources are surfaced.

voiceover.transcript: Because if we really start to use these tools across the board, the pedagogy bends around what the platform can do. Now, you see some example of this in say when you implement a VLE, Moodle or Blackboard or it might be Canvas, that when there's a set number of things you can do, those becomes the things you can do. It's a tautology, but it makes sense. Perhaps before VLE is lots more variation of what people were doing. Now, VLEs offer tremendous value. I'm not saying we don't use VLEs, but the same thing applies. If we're using a consumer or enterprise grade business tool to do educational tasks, the scope of what it's good at and what it's able to do will not be aligned with what we wanted to do.

voiceover_history.transcript: And as an example, again, for Mernhart, as a teaching and learning-focused academic, when we have set tools, the pedagogy starts to bend around those tools and what a platform can do and what a platform can measure. And there's standardization is an inevitable output of that. You can see this when we're using Moodle or Blackboard, you know, there used to be perhaps many more different, varied, weird and wonderful types of assessment, but when it has to be collapses, now there are different benefits to a VLE versus pen and paper and where before, but there is that standardization pressure and using these tools will only allow certain things. And so those are the things that will get done. Also, we're coming at this from getting these tools from the Silicon Valley, a big tech company where they want to measure anything. Analytics is a real selling point of these things, but these are surface metrics. OK, usage, who logged on and how long were they on for? Not what they were doing, what they learned, was there sort of any aha moments, all these things, right?

slides.heading: Priceless educational data will be captured unless it is governed
slides.body: Universities sit on rich corpora of writing, interaction and workflow data. Once AI layers are integrated, those traces become commercially valuable far beyond their immediate educational use unless institutions treat them as governed assets.

notes.transition: Now bring in extraction.
points: Educational data does not only support services; it can be pulled into upstream value capture.
Explain behavioural exhaust, model improvement, upstream value capture and governance in plain institutional terms.
The room should hear that data governance is about political economy, not only compliance.
Behavioural exhaust: Prompts, drafts, clicks, revisions and case interactions all become telemetry.
Upstream capture: Value created from public educational activity can be captured upstream and turned into model advantage elsewhere.
Governed asset: Separate operational delivery, evaluation and model-improvement uses rather than letting them blur together.

voiceover_history.transcript: And this last one is really, really important. So we are sitting on a goldmine of educational data. And this is what is termed by the industry as behavioral exhaust, which is both bafflingly dystopian and also vaguely funny. So it's behavioral exhaust as in everything we do is being recorded and everything done in AI is being recorded. And this is of amazing value. And I think this is something where if you take anything away from today, think about every single bit of work you've done at a university, every single bit of work a student's done at university, that whole journey, all the drafts, all the submissions, all the feedback, that is incredibly rich data with which to help build AI systems that continue that system, right? But instead of utilizing that data, we are freely giving it to large tech companies for no apparent reason, right? And they are using that data as value is being captured upstream. We are paying for the privilege handsomely when we could in fact be using that money to help in certain cases build our own models to do our own thing. Okay, if you want to summarize an email, use a commercial model, right? But if you want to really understand learning gain, right, for instance, or anything from pedagogy, we need to use all this data we have, the expertise we have already. And this data, this behavioral exhaust, this educational data, which we are sitting on a goldmine of should be a governed, strongly protected asset of our universities.

voiceover.transcript: So my last bit on dependence is a real bugbear of mine, right? We are sitting on a goldmine of data, of educational, pedagogic data. We have all the assessment definitions, the rubrics, the submissions themselves, and millions of students' submissions over the years, and we are just giving this away. But actually worse than that, we're paying for the privilege of giving it to a company to basically ignore it at best, right? And what we can be doing is so much more with that data. Rather than giving it to an upstream provider, we should be treating it as a governed asset that we control, that we use, however we want to use it, to our ends, our institutional, philosophical ends as higher education.

notes.transition: After the data implication, move from effects to process.
points: This section explains how convenience hardens into dependence over time.
Say that platforms rarely begin with a visible squeeze; they usually begin with generosity.
The audience should recognise a lifecycle, not a conspiracy.

voiceover.transcript: So looking back at history, how will we find ourselves when we depend on these companies? Because when hopefully when I put it like we are paying for the privilege of giving away our big assets, you know we don't want to do that. So how come we get caught in these loops of being captured and dependent on these technology providers?

voiceover_history.transcript: Now, in the next section, I'll talk about, okay, why am I so wary of these big companies? And that's because we look to history, right? These tech companies, there's a playbook, and it's not necessarily through bias or sometimes it's through malice. It's more through a nature of VC-backed tech companies.

slides.heading: Convenience becomes dependence in stages
slides.body: Platform capture rarely begins with a visible squeeze. It begins with generous access and genuine usefulness, then shifts toward buyer logic, monetisation and harder exit once the institution has adapted.

notes.transition: Start the lifecycle as a sequence rather than a series of separate beats.
points: Early-stage platforms often feel like public goods: low prices, credits, rapid integration and real usefulness.
Over time optimisation shifts toward telemetry, enterprise buyers and monetisation.
By the time the squeeze is felt, the institution has often already adapted around the platform.
Generosity: Subsidised access, rapid value and soft terms make experimentation feel costless.
Squeeze: Pricing, feature access and strategic direction change once dependence is deeper.
Lock-in: Workflows, habits and data structures make leaving much harder than it looked at the start.

voiceover.transcript: Well, again, it's back to my previous point, again, related to the boom. They are incredibly generous, right? And capture with technology firms happens with considerable generosity, right? But it's not always coming to an end eventually. And you know, they are selling at a loss to capture market share. And then the squeeze starts, the prices start to rise, by which point you are committed, and you've got no other options, and then you are locked in. And this has happened throughout the history of technology. And the more modern internet platforms is just a modus operandi. And I think it's really important to see that these things are great, but we have to try and avoid being locked into one platform or one vendor.

voiceover_history.transcript: The first part is that they have a lot of money, right? They have a lot of capital, venture capital, and they have to expand and get big quickly. So they're very generous, right? Be this with free version of chat GPT, which is basically burning money. This is Uber, right? When they move to a city, they're very cheap. And then once they have basically taken over, the price is all ramping up. Because then you've got the squeeze, okay, the rise of prices, reduce the options, things become paid add-ons, right? They've got you for longer and longer contracts, and you're basically locked in. And this is the standard playbook. And again, not necessarily done intentionally, although frequently is, but it's just a nature of their business. They have all this money, and sometime, at some point, they've got to start turning those handles, turning that ratchet to get out some profit, and it inevitably happens.

slides.heading: Path dependence becomes governance
slides.body: Early choices about tools, contracts and norms do not stay local or temporary. They accumulate into architecture. Standards harden, investments compound and alternatives narrow, so procurement quietly becomes governance.

notes.transition: Name the mechanism.
points: This is path dependence at institutional scale.
Today's local decisions accumulate into tomorrow's constrained options.
Say plainly that procurement becomes governance because procurement shapes architecture.
Standards harden: File formats, APIs, assessment rules and reporting practices settle around early winners.
Investment compounds: Training, integrations and custom workflows make the original choice seem ever more rational.
Options narrow: Code, market structure and institutional norms together shape what later choices still feel realistic.

voiceover.transcript: And this can happen quite slowly and that I'm really noticing is that norms become standards and they harden and institutional investment compounds. So, okay, we have a big contract with OpenAI. We've invested lots of training to use their specific tools, their specific way of doing things, right? At which point, okay, we're committed and our options narrow.

voiceover_history.transcript: And this is insidious because that generosity might seem great initially, but it means standards are set early, right? Any investment on their part and in our part compounds and we get more and more locked in and our options narrow, right? You get less and less flexibility. The more we buy into Anthropic or OpenAI or Microsoft Copilot, whatever it might be, okay, our options get narrower and narrower because we're just building, we're investing more, more knowledge, more, more effort on this one tool. Why spread ourselves thinly when we want to concentrate on one thing? I make the argument we should be spreading ourselves more thinly.

slides.heading: Prefer modular architecture and plural ecosystems
slides.body: The goal is not to build everything locally. It is to avoid allowing one stack to become the only imaginable path.

notes.transition: Turn the right of exit into an architectural principle.
points: Do not let one stack become the only imaginable route through the institution.
Modularity and plural ecosystems preserve bargaining power, resilience and room to adapt.
This is how you keep options open before convenience hardens into culture.

voiceover_history.transcript: So what should we do instead? Think about modular design. We should be using all the providers. I use a ton of AI in my spin-out company, from coding, especially to designing models or to data science, and I use all the main providers. I have accounts with all of them. They're all good at different things. Different models are good at different things. I have some magentic workflows that use open AI and anthropic models and open source models all at the same time for very different things for different speeds, right? We should have that exposure, be able to pick the best thing, right? Have the right size model. We shouldn't be using just what's given to us, be it some frankly crappy co-pilot model or some huge frontier model that's very wasteful. We should decide what's right for us, and we should experiment and have the ability to plug and play with the different models that are given by providers or as time goes on and the open source models become much more prevalent. Perhaps those open source models are even better, are fine-tuned versions of those models, fine-tuned on all that really rich educational data that we've got.

voiceover.transcript: So what should we do instead? Instead, we should prefer a modular design. We should not be committing to one provider. We should have the option to move between providers, to have our own models, right? Give ourselves that optionality, right? The more different types of models, different sizes of models we are exposed to, the better because it keeps our options open and keeps people understanding that, okay, we don't have to buy this in from one of the Frontier Labs. Instead, we can build aspects ourselves, right? We can use these tools. We can use the hardware, the open source models, the expertise we have in the university, right? To actually build what we need.

slides.title: A different strategy
slides.subtitle: Engaged stewardship instead of passive consumption

notes.transition: Now gather the earlier implications into a more coherent strategy.
points: The audience has already seen governance, procurement, data and architecture implications earlier in the talk.
This section consolidates those scattered implications into an explicit stewardship posture.
The answer remains neither refusal nor surrender; it is now being named more fully.

voiceover.transcript: Okay, so how do we go about this? So hopefully I've laid out the key issues with what's happening with the AI boom, what happens with capture by these tech companies, what the options are around sleepwalking into these issues, so what could we do differently?

voiceover_history.transcript: So Stuart, you've talked about how bad these companies are, you talked about how much data we have, the possibilities and resources we have, the expertise we have, what we should be doing, right? How can we actually do this? What would be the

slides.heading: The strongest response is neither refusal nor surrender
slides.body: Universities do not need a fantasy of total sovereignty, and they should not accept passive dependence either. The workable position is engaged stewardship.

notes.transition: State the third position clearly.
points: The strongest response is engaged stewardship.
Say that universities may buy some things, build or host some things, adapt some things, and refuse some things.
The important point is doing that from deliberate governance rather than drift.
Buy some things: Use commercial infrastructure where it is genuinely useful and where the terms are governable.
Build or host selectively: Keep local or sector-controlled options for high-value or high-risk uses where continuity, privacy or contestability matter most.
The point is not to recreate frontier labs; it is to hold enough capability to negotiate, evaluate and keep critical educational functions under institutional control.
Refuse some things: Reject deployments that undermine educational purpose even if they look efficient on paper.

voiceover.transcript: So, for me, the strongest response isn't pure refusal to use these things, and it's also not just pure surrender to a vendor, there's somewhere in between. I do believe that these Frontier Labs can really help us, right? The tools they sell us can help us. So we do buy some things. It will make more sense to buy where it's useful for our workflow, where there's an overlap between our use case and the consumer and the business slash enterprise use case. We should use those tools, right? That makes sense. But there's a large section where the use case is very different. We want control and ownership, and we want to set the direction aligned with our university's principles, and that's where we should build or host our own local models selectively. Now, we're not talking about training frontier models here, billions of dollars. These open source models exist. I've got them running on this laptop now, right? You can play with them after. Everyone wants to have a chat. I can give you some tips of how to get started on this. It's not difficult. The models exist, and we can very quickly build our tools, because in universities, we have an incredible asset at our disposal, and I'll reiterate this a few times, because we'll have access to the hardware, we have access to open source models, we have access to expertise. I'm not just talking about computer scientists here, but social scientists, educational researchers, right? And we also have all of the data needed to help with this, which I'll touch on a little bit later. So we have the ability to do these things. Also, finally, I think it's really important that we have the option to refuse to use AI in certain cases. Again, we don't have to use it across the board. Anyone pushing AI across everything is frankly being a bit silly, because there are aspects where not using AI is actually better, especially when it comes to educational outcomes, student experience, things like that. We have to be very selective, and you can really put people off by automating away any human contact.

voiceover_history.transcript: So, first of all, I would say a strong response is neither total refusal, I don't really have much time for people to say no AI at all, I don't think that's sensible or helpful. I also don't think we should surrender to a big company, get all our branded t-shirts on and just go, kumbaya, let's go open AI on Thropic, something in between. So we want to buy some things, right? These tools are extremely useful, useful tools are worth money, we should buy some, right? But we should be very selective with what we buy, not lock ourselves in and not let them dictate how we use it and what it's used for. We find a use case, we get the right tool for the job. We should also build or host our own AI, right, selectively. This is a big one, this is something no one's doing. As a sector, we have the expertise, we have the data, especially collectively, if I've learned anything from my work in student voice, is that these models get so much better when there is multiple institutions involved. It's a much richer data set, a much wider distribution of data. So we can build and host selectively. And we can also, for those people who want to refuse things, refuse some things. We don't have to use it for everything. We can just say, no, we're keeping that analog, human, for these reasons, based on our principles as an institution. I think that is entirely defensible.

slides.heading: Start from public-interest goals, not adoption goals
slides.body: An AI strategy should define what the university is trying to protect and improve before it defines what tools it plans to deploy.

notes.transition: Start the constructive section from purpose.
points: Strategy has to begin with what the university is trying to protect and improve, not with tool adoption targets.
Use human contact, inclusion, and educational purpose as guardrails.
This keeps the policy agenda tied to educational purpose rather than IT modernisation alone.
Human contact: Use AI to create more time for mentoring, feedback, discussion and care.
Inclusion and accessibility: Prioritise translation, captioning, alternative formats and scaffolded support.
Educational purpose: Explicitly reject headcount reduction or content automation in place of teaching as primary aims.

voiceover.transcript: So, we should really start from a position of, okay, how do we decide what to buy, what to refuse, and what to build ourselves?
