Think about, okay, what are the human elements we want to maintain? What are the key aspects for us, our principles in what we want to maintain? Now, for me, throughout my career, it's been about inclusion and accessibility, and these AI tools can really help with that if designed and deployed in the right way, right? So, we think about our purpose, our educational purpose, and we lean into that, and that is our starting point, right? That is our lens through which we decide to buy or not refuse or build ourselves.

voiceover_history.transcript: So what might some of those institutional goals be, or principles? One is that human contact is important, right? Okay, we're not bringing ourselves down to purely, okay, this personnel knows more stuff, kicks them out the door. It's about building human relationships, trust, right? Human contact is important, right? And AI can help with that, but it has to be very on the edges, right? We want to maximize human contact. We want to have a goal of maximizing inclusion and accessibility. These tools can really help with that, right? There are lots of people who find information far more accessible, right, through these AI tools, and they can be more inclusive, right, if used correctly, if they are matched to the educational purpose, right? That's the ultimate principle, especially on the educational side, right? That we have these goals, we have to meet those rather than saying, okay, we're only using AI by whatever date. Instead, it's like, how can we push forward our goals?

slides.text: Make evaluation a core institutional capability

notes.transition: Then move to evaluation.
points: Evaluation is the quiet centrepiece of stewardship.
Say that universities cannot rely on demos, generic benchmarks, or marketing claims for high-stakes uses.
Stress task-based testing, independent scrutiny, and continuous review.
Task-based testing: Build benchmark tasks for feedback, retrieval, advising, accessibility and disciplinary use cases.
Independent scrutiny: Review hallucination risk, bias, privacy leakage, citation behaviour and fail-safe performance.
Continuous review: Re-evaluate when underlying models, terms or integrations change upstream.

voiceover_history.transcript: So key to any strategy, right? Key to what I've said before, buy some things, build some things and refuse some things, is make evaluation a core institutional capability. We will not know what to buy, what to build, what to refuse, if we don't have any way to measure the efficacy of these models. So evaluation is key. There's something I've learned building these models for decades, is that it's all about evaluation, all about the test dataset, all about the gold standard dataset you've hand labeled, that is what you need. You need to be able to evaluate these things. And evaluation can happen individually, but it can also happen collectively.

voiceover.transcript: So here comes a key point in the talk, where I talked before about the assets capabilities we have. So we have access to this post-boom hardware. We have access to these open source LLM models. We have access to expertise within the university. I have access to the data. Now, it's those last two combined that we need to really think about, because we want to make evaluation a core institutional capability. The ability to say, is this model any good or not for our use case? Then we can decide, are we buying from the consumer way, from the enterprise way? Are we building? How are we testing? How good those things are? We're using lots of different models. How do we go about saying which ones are best? Evaluation should become a key, key capability, and it's something we don't do much of at all. Now, evals, as they're known in the sector, are really common. That's how these models are made, right? These models are made better by being able to test them and improve them over time, and it's something we just don't do, but we have everything needed to build that capability, so it should become a core competence of universities to be able to evaluate these models against our requirements.

slides.heading: Sector collaboration and public alternatives matter
slides.body: No single university has enough leverage to negotiate every clause, evaluate every model or build every critical service alone. Collaboration is a practical necessity, including where the sector decides to build some capacity for itself.

notes.transition: Finish the section by scaling outward.
points: Single universities are too small to negotiate, evaluate, and build everything alone.
Sector collaboration and public alternatives are not idealism; they are a realistic response to asymmetric markets.
This lets you sound practical without sounding institutionally isolated.
Shared procurement: Use sector bodies and consortia to negotiate stronger data terms, exit rights and auditability.
Shared evaluation: Build common test suites, model cards and red-teaming practices for educational use cases.

Public-interest infrastructure: Explore national, regional or mission-based alternatives where the market produces choke points, including shared hosting or model-building where that is strategically sensible.

voiceover_history.transcript: So that collectivism, I think, is important. We can share things, right? We can share procurement. Why don't, in terms of, okay, we get a joint contract to use a certain provider, right? But also perhaps more importantly, we could build some of these tools ourselves, right? We have all the expertise needed, right? And we're not just talking, okay, the computer science department, right? We're talking education department, the social sciences department, the admin experience, the transformation teams, procurement, everything, right? We have all of the people, every university, and joined together, we could build something spectacular if we put our minds to it, right? There's nothing stopping us, right? The stage is set. There'll be hardware all there. The boom will come. The bubble will burst. The hardware will be left. We're in a great position with these fantastic open source models, all this available hardware, experience using these tools, massive evaluation data set, right? To build these valuations, to say, okay, this is what we want to do. This is how we can know if it's good or not, right?

voiceover.transcript: Now, we don't have to do all that evaluation ourselves. So something I've learned from student voice AI and working with many institutions across the UK is that people are willing to share anonymous data in order to improve the outcomes for the university. So we can do shared evaluation. This helps us making those build, buy, or refuse decisions, which should be really quite granular. I don't think we should be buying into one vendor or refusing all vendors. Because I think we have to be deciding on a case-by-case basis what to do in a joined up way. But evaluation helps with those decisions of build, buy, or refuse. Also helps us when these models will change. Part of that problem of that capture, that squeeze, the initial generosity, then things get squeezed. Frequently, they'll bump you down to a cheaper model, and you see this all the time if you use these tools a lot. There are regressions. They'll say, OK, this is still the model, but they've made it more efficient, they've made it smaller, with some performance loss. If we have a large shared evaluation data set, we can know when that happens and go, no, no, we're not doing that, we're doing this other option, or we use a different model. We have visibility, and it brings some rigor to the whole thing. And that's on us, right? But we have to have that evaluation capability, and we map that to our principles. And we can group these things nationally, by regional groups, or by mission, or size, whatever it might be. All the groupings need to happen, the shared evaluation, but I really think that's something really positive to aim for.

slides.heading: Ownership means owning some of the stack
slides.body: Contracts matter, but universities do not really shape their AI future if every consequential layer remains external. Ownership in practice means deciding which parts of compute, models and oversight must stay institutionally or sector governed.

notes.transition: Make ownership concrete rather than abstract.
points: Say that ownership is not only about procurement clauses; it is also about who controls key layers of the system.
The claim is not that every university should own the entire stack.
The claim is that some compute, some models and some evaluation capacity should be institutionally or sector governed where mission, resilience or contestability matter.
Compute where it matters: Retain local or shared hosting for high-value functions where continuity, privacy or bargaining power are strategically important.
Models for university tasks: Build, adapt or fine-tune models for bounded educational and administrative use cases rather than relying only on general-purpose systems.
Evaluation under our control: Keep benchmark design, red-teaming and monitoring in hands that are accountable to educational values.

voiceover_history.transcript: And that means taking some ownership of this, right? And that might be ownership of different aspects, layers of the stack. Okay, in terms of compute, that might not be owning our own data center, but it'll be having contracts with people like AWS to help power that, right? It's absolutely great. We'll be looking to own some models, right? Collectively, I think would be the best way to do this. And again, this is ambitious, right? But people said that about student voice AI as well, that universities would never share their data and multiple universities have for the better good. I think this could work, right? And importantly, that big evaluation data set is under our control. We decide if it's doing the thing we want it to do or not, right? We get to decide if this works, so yay or nay. Not someone else, not some product manager who has very different incentives to us, right? We get to decide.

voiceover.transcript: So we have to take ownership and responsibility for our decisions and which AI providers we're using. Again, in that build, buy and refuse, we have to decide what compute to use where, which cloud providers to use. We have to decide which, hopefully, university-specific models to use or develop or build collectively. We have to continually evaluate this process. This isn't going to be a choice we can make. It's going to be much harder than saying, okay, we go with open AI. Thank you very much. Instead, we need to have that rigor and build that evaluation set and use that to define, okay, we have this evaluation data set of everything we've done before, everything we want to do, right? And it inherently encodes all of our institutional priorities because it's what we've been doing, right? Use that to evaluate these models.

slides.text: The alternative to passive dependence is not heroic self-sufficiency. It is practical stewardship.

notes.transition: State the positive identity of the argument.
points: The alternative to passive dependence is practical stewardship.
This line matters because it keeps the keynote from sounding like a sovereignty fantasy.
Pause slightly; this is the constructive counterpart to the earlier warnings.

voiceover.transcript: So, what I'm not saying here is that we have to do everything ourselves. It's a common failure mode across universities to want to do everything ourselves, do everything in-house, where there should be real benefit to outsourcing some things, right? So, if you need a tool to summarize an email, I'm not sure why you would, but perhaps some people want that, give that to one of these models, absolutely fine. But when we're looking at key functions as mapped to our principles as an institution, right, education being one of them, research being the other one, we have to be really careful what we're doing. So, we don't want to be entirely self-sufficient and sort of cutting off our nose to spite our face. We also don't want to be passively dependent on these Silicon Valley companies. Instead, we want to be practical stewards of our data, of our models, and decide what we want to build, what we're looking to buy, and what we're looking to refuse, because again, that third option is there, and we have to do that on a case-by-case basis. There should be some of each, right? And currently, we're vacillating between buying everything, refusing everything, and also not building anything, when I think there's a far healthier mix in there aligned with our principles as an institution.

voiceover_history.transcript: But what I'm not saying is here, is that we should build everything ourself. This is, again, a common trap in higher education, is that, okay, we'll build our own thing, and it'll be really tight and just kept up. No. The alternative to sort of becoming entirely passive independent on these big companies is not doing everything ourselves. It's practical stewardship, using the hardware that will be available, using the open source tools that will be available, working together to build something where it's not being supplied by these vendors. Again, if you want to summarize an email, there's simple models for that, but there's more complex things around research, administration, especially education, right? We want to be in control.

But to be clear, what I'm not saying here is we do everything ourselves, because again, that's a real trap the universities fall into is not invented here syndrome, we build our own tools that is held together by bits of string, and really fragile. So we're not being heroic is self sufficient here. And we're also not being pathfully dependent on a Silicon Valley tech company. We have to be practical stewards of our approach of our data of our, of our students of our institutions. Yeah, cool.

slides.title: What the next decade looks like
slides.subtitle: Two futures are still possible

notes.transition: Move from strategy to consequences.
points: This final section asks what the next decade looks like under two different patterns of choice.
Use the subtitle to frame the end as a genuine fork, not a foregone conclusion.
The tone here should become slightly more civic and decision-oriented.

voiceover.transcript: So to wrap this up, what might the next decade look like? Now, I'm not going to talk technically and things like that, although I'll happily pontificate over a beer later on if anyone wants to discuss where these models are going. But it's, I want to give you some options of, okay, what's a positive scenario and what's a potential negative scenario? What might this look like, right? Try and give you an idea.

voiceover_history.transcript: So in this last section, I'd like to give you some scenarios of what a good outcome might look like and what a less good outcome might look like.

slides.section: What the next decade looks like
slides.heading: If universities play this moment well
slides.body: The optimistic future is not one in which universities beat hyperscalers at their own game. It is one in which they use the boom to build durable, plural and publicly oriented capability.

notes.transition: Paint the better future concretely.
points: A good future is not universities beating hyperscalers at their own game.
It is cheaper infrastructure on better terms, human-centred pedagogy, and stronger public capability.
Keep the picture specific enough to feel plausible rather than utopian.
Cheaper infrastructure, better terms: Institutions inherit mature tools and compute without surrendering all bargaining power.
Human-centred pedagogy: AI is used to deepen feedback, accessibility and high-value contact rather than thin them out.
Stronger public capacity: Sector collaboration creates standards, evaluation routines and alternatives that keep the system contestable.

voiceover.transcript: So, if we play this moment well, right, post-bubble, right, there'll be cheap infrastructure, right, that we can utilize to build our own models, to do our own thing. That becomes a real option. It's possible now. It becomes more and more possible as we go on. We can build these systems aligned with students, a human-centered pedagogy, as in teaching and learning needs very specific models. We can build them. We can decide what then. We have the expertise in universities. We have all the data we need. We can build a really robust evaluation so we know if it's actually working or not. Again, academic rigor, right? We bring that to the table. And what this could lead to is we could be an example of, okay, a set of public bodies that work together to build their own AI tools. And this could be a real example, right, across the public sector and beyond.

voiceover_history.transcript: What makes a good outcome look like? So, post-boom, we have cheaper infrastructure, better terms, more flexibility, right? We have the ability to have all these models help with human-centered pedagogy, right? Rather than shoving things down people's throats on these consumer models, instead, we design models based on educational research and best practices to help people learn, right? We also have the opportunity, as universities, because we have all of these strengths, we have everything in place. Like I say, we'll have access to the post-boom hardware, we'll have access to open-source models. We've got all the expertise, of course, computer science, social science, admin, transformation, right? We've got this huge amount of data, this evaluation data set we can use. That's everything you need to create a literally world-class model. We could show the way for public sector bodies, groups, right, using these tools for our own principles, for our own direction.

slides.section: What the next decade looks like
slides.heading: If universities drift into platform dependency
slides.body: The pessimistic future is not spectacular collapse. It is a quieter form of dependency in which institutions keep functioning while their freedom, pluralism and educational quality erode.

notes.transition: Then contrast it with the quieter bad future.
points: The dependency future is not dramatic collapse; it is ongoing functioning under worsening external control.
Emphasise rent extraction, thinner provision, and opaque governance.
The point is to show what slow loss of autonomy looks like in ordinary institutional life.
Rent extraction: AI becomes a permanent premium layer across teaching, admin, assessment and research workflows.
Thin provision: Lower-status students and institutions get automated, standardised provision while wealthier parts of the sector preserve human-rich models.
Opaque governance: Research, pedagogy and student support are increasingly shaped by systems the university cannot fully audit or challenge.

voiceover_history.transcript: But what else could happen? If we just buy into these consumer or enterprise contracts, these models designed for businesses or consumers are not designed for our use cases in universities, then we get locked in. We are basically paying rent to use these things. We're giving away all of our data. The options get less and less, and we have none of that transparency, none of that visibility into what is being created, what is being delivered, how these models are changing over time. Basically, a very bad situation, and that's equally possible.

slides.section: What the next decade looks like
slides.heading: The next few years require three decisions from leaders
slides.body: Every institution does not need the same answer, but all of them need to decide what they will refuse to outsource by default.

points.title: What must we retain control over?
What should we build or evaluate together?

notes.transition: Convert the contrast into leadership choices.
points: Every institution has to decide what it will refuse to outsource by default.
Use the three questions as decision tests: what must we retain control over, what should we build or evaluate together, and what rights should students have?
This sets up the closing agenda cleanly.
What must we retain control over?: Define the teaching, assessment, data or support functions where exit, auditability and human oversight are non-negotiable.
What should we build or evaluate together?: Identify the areas where sector collaboration creates more leverage than acting alone.
What rights should students have?: Make explicit the rights to transparency, contestability, accessibility and non-AI pathways in core processes.

voiceover.transcript: So fundamentally, the next few years require three big decisions. Is that what do we want to retain control over? What aspects of AI are we wanting to control? Because I believe there are aspects we should be controlling. Again, we buy some things, but we build and control some things too. Also, of course, for the refusedics, also refuse some things. There's no problem about saying, this thing is done old school. No problem at all. It's when you say, I'm not using any AI or any computers at all in my life, you become slightly extreme, and you're really cutting off your nose to spite your face. So what should we build or evaluate together? And importantly, what rights should students have? What rights should the people involved in this process? They don't want to be taught by an AI bot. If they did, they could go in and do that. It comes down to being lecture recordings. It's like, you could just be taught by on YouTube. Yeah, you could. You could absolutely get a world-class education watching YouTube. You could learn as an aerospace engineer, a PhD. You could get an M.Eng. aerospace just by watching YouTube and just taking some exams. But people don't want to do that. That's not how most people learn. If you can learn that way, if you're an autodidact, go for it. That's not how students want to learn. So how do students want to learn? What rights should they have around their data, what models they're exposed to? That's really important.

slides.section: What the next decade looks like
slides.text: Universities are not only deciding whether to use AI. They are helping fix the defaults of higher education for years.

notes.transition: Lift back from the checklist to the stakes.
points: The point is bigger than whether to deploy a particular tool.
Universities are helping set the defaults that will structure higher education for years.
This is the right slide to widen the frame before the final question.

voiceover.transcript: So what might the next decade look like? Well, we as universities aren't deciding, okay, should we use AI or not, right? We're deciding what are the defaults, right, for the next decades, okay? What is education going to look like, right? Either we get to decide, or someone's going to decide for us.

slides.text: We have all the pieces to build and control our own AI systems, so what are we going to build?

notes.transition: End with the question rather than another answer.
points: Let the closing question hang for a moment and hand neatly into discussion or panel.
The point is to force the room to connect infrastructure control with the kind of university it wants AI to serve.
Do not rush the last line; it is the civic and institutional landing point.

voiceover.transcript: And a final closing question for you all, something to go away and think about, if AI becomes part of higher education infrastructure, who should control it? And what kind of universities should it serve? Universities will change, right? But I believe the best change will be when we control the AI and use AI to make the change ourselves, retain that autonomy. Thank you very much.
